q: Fluid statics concentrates on fluids in motion.
The study of the flow of liquid is called hydrodynamics.
Surface tension and capillarity are important concepts of hydrodynamics.
The volume of a gas depends on pressure and temperature obeying Boyle's law and Charles' law.
The volume of a liquid depends heavily on pressure if the temperature is constant.
Density of an incompressible liquid is constant for any pressure provided that the temperature is kept constant.
In steady laminar flow, streamlines cross each other frequently.
In steady laminar flow, every fluid particle arriving at a given point has the same velocity.
In turbulent flow, the direction of the fluid particles is always steady and linear.
Ideal laminar fluid flow considers that the flow has no friction or friction is neglected.
Internal friction in fluids is called dynamic density.
Sea breeze is an example of turbulent flow and storm is a laminar flow.
For an ideal fluid flowing through a pipe, the mass flowing in section 1 must equal the mass flowing in section 2.
The equation of continuity states that the liquid entering one end of the pipe leaves from the other end with a variable volume flow rate.
The larger the area of the pipe, the faster is the speed.
The speed of water spraying from a hose increases by making the hose area smaller.
Bernoulli's equation relates the pressure, flow speed, and height for the steady flow of an ideal compressible fluid.
Bernoulli's equation was first derived in 1738 by the Swiss mathematician Daniel Bernoulli.
According to the work-energy principle, the work done on a section of fluid must equal the change in total mechanical energy.
In Bernoulli's derivation, the work done $\Delta W$ is equal to $(p_1 - p_2)\Delta V$.
The change in kinetic energy in Bernoulli's equation is given by $\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2}\rho\Delta V(v_2^2 - v_1^2)$.
The change in potential energy in Bernoulli's equation is given by $\Delta PE = \rho\Delta V g(h_1 - h_2)$.
Bernoulli's theorem states that the sum of pressure energy, kinetic energy and potential energy per unit volume remains constant.
The air flow moving above the top curved surface of an aeroplane wing has a shorter distance to travel than the lower flat surface.
High velocity of air flow along the top surface of a wing causes a low pressure above the wing.
The pressure difference between the lower and upper surfaces of an aeroplane wing provides a lift on the wing.
When the piston of an insect sprayer is pressed, the liquid inside the container rushes up because it is at a lower pressure than the nozzle.
The Magnus effect is named after the German physicist Heinrich Gustav Magnus, who described the effect in 1852.
During cyclones, the reduced pressure inside the house causes the roof to fly away.
Most of the fluids are ideal ones and offer no resistance to motion.
Viscosity arises when there is relative motion between layers of the fluid.
A fluid with large viscosity resists motion because its strong intermolecular forces give it a lot of internal friction.
A fluid with low viscosity flows with great difficulty because its molecular make up results in very high friction.
The viscosity of liquids increases rapidly with an increase in temperature.
The viscosity of gases increases with an increase in temperature.
Viscosity is an extensive property because it changes as the amount of matter changes.
The SI unit of viscosity is pascal second ($\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$).
The unit symbol $\text{N}\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}$ stands for newton second per metre squared.
Kinematic viscosity is used to measure fluid's resistance to flow when an external force is applied.
Dynamic viscosity is also known as absolute viscosity.
Kinematic viscosity is more useful than dynamic viscosity to measure the resistive flow of a fluid under the weight of gravity.
Newton's law of viscosity states that shear stress is directly proportional to the velocity gradient.
In symbols, shear stress is represented by the Greek letter $\eta$.
Shear stress is the shearing force per unit volume.
Viscosity is defined as the ratio of shearing stress to the velocity gradient.
The formula for volume flow rate used in the continuity equation examples is $\frac{V}{t} = Av$.
The term $\frac{dv}{dy}$ in Newton's law of viscosity represents the pressure gradient.
In the equation $\tau = -\eta \frac{dv}{dy}$, the negative sign shows the direction of the shearing force.
Aviation is an application of fluid dynamics that deals with motion underneath the surface of water.
The density of air remains constant in the room heating duct problem of Example 3.2.
Viscosity is defined as the ratio of velocity gradient to the shearing stress.
Stokes' law expresses the drag force resisting the fall of small spherical objects through a fluid medium under the influence of gravity.
The drag force acting upward in resistance to the fall of a spherical object in a viscous liquid increases as the velocity becomes constant at terminal velocity.
In the formula for the weight of a sphere ball, $w = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_1 g$, the variable $\rho_1$ is the density of the liquid.
At a constant velocity of fall, the upward and downward forces acting on the spherical object are in balance.
Surface tension is the reason a water strider is able to stride along water surface without sinking.
Paperclips, razor blades and light coins are unable to rest on water if their density is higher than that of water.
The SI unit of surface tension is newton per metre ($N m^{-1}$).
Surface tension can also be referred to as a force because it is the tension between liquid molecules.
Cohesion refers to forces between molecules of different substances.
Adhesion refers to forces between molecules of different substances.
Cohesion makes water molecules pull together making the water surface to stretch like an elastic skin.
Capillary action is defined as the spontaneous flow of a liquid into a narrow tube or porous material.
Capillary action is caused solely by cohesive forces of the liquid.
In order for wicking to occur, a tube needs to be sufficiently large in diameter.
The rise of water underneath the ground is an example of capillary action.
Capillary action helps bringing water up into the roots of plants.
When a glass tube with a small inside diameter is dipped into water, the water undergoes depression and falls inside the tube.
Liquids that rise in small bore tubes inserted into the liquid are said to wet the tube.
Liquids that are depressed within thin tubes below the surface of the surrounding liquid wet the tube.
Mercury is a liquid that wets glass capillary tubes.
Water level rises in a glass tube until there is a sufficient mass of liquid for gravitational forces to overcome the intermolecular forces.
The actual amount of capillary rise depends on the surface tension $\gamma$, the contact angle $\theta$ and the radius $r$ of the tube.
If fluid particles are moving steady in smooth paths in layers, with each layer moving smoothly past the adjacent layers with mixing, such a flow is called laminar flow.
If the flow or path of the fluid particles are irregular, their direction is always changing or whirling; this fluid movement is called turbulent flow.
Bernoulli's theorem states that the sum of pressure, kinetic energy per unit volume and potential energy per unit volume remains constant for a compressible, viscous fluid.
The resistance to fluid motion (or) internal resistance of fluid is called viscosity.
A flat circular plate has two rims (outer and inner rims) in contact with the liquid surface.
A horizontal circular loop of wire has two rims (outer and inner rims) in contact with the liquid surface.
A needle has two edges in contact with the liquid surface when placed gently on it.
